title: 5 Sequels That Straight-Up Beat The Original Movie (According To Actual Critics, Not Just Vibes)
dek: "The sequel was better" is usually fighting talk — but these five times the Tomatometer and Metacritic itself backed it up.

items.heading: The Dark Knight Didn't Just Beat Batman Begins, It Ran It Out Of Gotham
items.text: Batman Begins rebooted the franchise to strong reviews, but Christopher Nolan's follow-up turned Batman into a full-blown crime epic and critics noticed immediately. The Dark Knight sits at a stellar 94% on Rotten Tomatoes, compared to Batman Begins' comparatively "human" 85%, and it's still the only pre-2012 superhero movie on RT's list of the 300 best films ever made.

images.caption: 94% and still climbing the all-time best-movies lists.
A very respectable 85% — just not Dark Knight numbers.

items.heading: Aliens Technically Out-Scored Alien And We're Still Processing This
items.text: Ridley Scott's Alien is a certified sci-fi horror masterpiece at 93% on Rotten Tomatoes — and then James Cameron came along, swapped slow-burn dread for full-tilt action, and edged it out at 94%. Critics have openly flagged the discrepancy, even while noting Alien still wins the franchise crown once audience scores get factored in.

images.caption: 94% — the marines-vs-xenomorphs sequel that out-scored the original.
A legendary 93%, just a hair behind its own sequel.

items.heading: Mad Max: Fury Road Left The Rest Of The Franchise In The Dust
items.text: The original Mad Max sits at a solid 90% on Rotten Tomatoes and even The Road Warrior — long considered the franchise's high point — tops out around 93-94%. Then, three decades later, George Miller returned with Fury Road and blew both away with a 97% critics' score and six Oscar wins, proof that "the fourth movie in an old franchise" doesn't have to mean diminishing returns.

images.caption: 97% — the franchise's highest-rated entry by a mile.
The gritty 90%-rated original that started it all.

items.heading: Star Trek II: The Wrath Of Khan Rescued The Franchise After A Critical Disaster
items.text: Star Trek: The Motion Picture landed with a thud at just 52% on Rotten Tomatoes, with its own critics' consensus calling it a movie whose "biggest villain is a cloud." Three years later Wrath of Khan showed up with actual stakes, an iconic villain, and a devastating ending, and critics rewarded it with 87% — a jump so big that Entertainment Weekly credited the film with saving Star Trek as a franchise.

images.caption: 87% and widely called the best Star Trek film ever made.
A rough 52% start for the franchise.

items.heading: Paddington 2 Briefly Became The Best-Reviewed Movie In Rotten Tomatoes History
items.text: The first Paddington was already a critical darling at 96-97%, but Paddington 2 topped it with a jaw-dropping 100% Tomatometer score that, for a few weeks in 2021, technically made it the single highest-rated movie on all of Rotten Tomatoes — ahead of Citizen Kane — before one lone negative review knocked it down to 99%. Either way, a kids' movie about a marmalade-loving bear out-reviewed basically all of cinema, sequel included.

images.caption: Briefly the #1 rated film ever on Rotten Tomatoes.
A beloved 96-97% start for the bear.
